content.text: Why Burn Rate Alerts Outperform Simple Threshold Alerts

A simple threshold alert on an SLI fires when the current value crosses a static threshold — for example, when availability drops below 99.9%. The problem is that this tells you nothing about urgency. An availability of 99.85% during a 5-minute window could be a transient blip that resolves on its own, or it could be the start of an outage that will exhaust the 30-day error budget within hours. The threshold alert treats both situations identically.

Burn rate alerts answer a different and more operationally relevant question: at the current rate of failure, how long until the error budget is exhausted? A burn rate of 14.4x means the 30-day budget will be gone in 2 days. A burn rate of 1x means it will last exactly 30 days. This framing converts an abstract metric into a concrete urgency: how much time do we have to act?

attrs.alt: Three burn rate alert tiers with multi-window configuration and response protocol

content.text: The 14.4x threshold is derived from 30 / 2 = 15, rounded to 14.4 to align with a 2-hour detection window (30 / (24 * 14.4) ≈ 2.08 days). The 6x threshold corresponds to a 5-day budget exhaustion (30 / 6 = 5). The 3x threshold corresponds to 10 days — enough time for a weekly review cycle to identify and plan a remediation.
Multi-Window Configuration: Why Two Windows Are Required

A single window for burn rate alerts creates a tradeoff between sensitivity and noise. A short window (5 minutes) catches acute outages quickly but fires on transient spikes that resolve without intervention — every brief connectivity blip becomes a P1 page. A long window (6 hours) avoids noise but confirms a slow burn only after significant budget has been consumed.

The solution is to require both windows to exceed the threshold simultaneously. The fast window confirms that the burn is active right now. The slow window confirms that it has been sustained — not a transient spike. If either window does not exceed the threshold, the alert does not fire:
Fast: active now. Slow: sustained for an hour.
Fast: not a brief blip. Slow: multi-hour burn.
Single: slow degradation over a full day.
Recording rules pre-compute the SLI values for each window. The burn rate alerts reference these pre-computed values, keeping the alert expressions readable and the evaluation fast:

# monitoring/burn_rate_alerts.yaml
# Multi-window burn rate alerts for the availability SLO (99.9% / 30-day).
# Error budget = 1 - 0.999 = 0.001 (0.1%)
groups:
  - name: genai.error_budget
    rules:
      # Tier 1: 14.4x burn — exhausts budget in ~2 days
      # Requires BOTH the 1h window AND the 5m window to exceed threshold.
      # Fast window prevents false pages on brief spikes.
      - alert: ErrorBudgetBurnRateCritical
        expr: |
          (
            (1 - sli:availability:ratio_1h) > (1 - 0.999) * 14.4
          ) and (
            (1 - sli:availability:ratio_5m) > (1 - 0.999) * 14.4
          )
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          slo: availability
          tier: '1'
        annotations:
          summary: 'Error budget burning at 14.4x — budget gone in ~2 days'
          description: |
            1h error rate: {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}.
            At this rate, the 30-day error budget will be exhausted
            in approximately 2 days.
          runbook_url: 'https://wiki/runbooks/error-budget-burn-critical.md'

      # Tier 2: 6x burn — exhausts budget in ~5 days
      # Requires 6h and 30m windows. Slower to confirm — avoids
      # paging on incidents that self-resolved within an hour.
      - alert: ErrorBudgetBurnRateHigh
        expr: |
          (
            (1 - sli:availability:ratio_6h) > (1 - 0.999) * 6
          ) and (
            (1 - sli:availability:ratio_30m) > (1 - 0.999) * 6
          )
        for: 15m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          slo: availability
          tier: '2'
        annotations:
          summary: 'Error budget burning at 6x — budget gone in ~5 days'
          runbook_url: 'https://wiki/runbooks/error-budget-burn-high.md'

      # Tier 3: 3x burn — exhausts budget in ~10 days
      # Single 1-day window. No immediate page — reviewed at next standup.
      - alert: ErrorBudgetBurnRateModerate
        expr: |
          (1 - sli:availability:ratio_1d) > (1 - 0.999) * 3
        for: 2h
        labels:
          severity: info
          slo: availability
          tier: '3'
        annotations:
          summary: 'Error budget burning at 3x — review at next standup'

      # Eval pass rate burn alert (different SLO structure)
      # SLO: >= 94% (7d avg). Budget = 6pp below 100%.
      - alert: EvalBudgetBurnRateHigh
        expr: |
          (0.94 - sli:eval_pass_rate:avg_7d) / (1 - 0.94) > 6
        for: 30m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          slo: eval_pass_rate
          tier: '2'
        annotations:
          summary: 'Eval pass rate budget burning at 6x'
          runbook_url: 'https://wiki/runbooks/eval-pass-rate-degradation.md'
The Tier 1 response is identical to a P1 incident response. The burn rate context tells the engineer how long they have, but the response process is the same:
Identify which SLO is burning from the alert label (availability, eval, latency). Open the corresponding runbook.
Determine if mitigation is possible without a code change (feature flag disable, traffic shedding). If yes, do it now.
Post first status update in the incident channel. If a code change is needed, escalate to the feature team.
Mitigation should be active or a rollback should be underway.
For GenAI services, the first mitigation action depends on the SLO that is burning:
Check LLM provider status; activate fallback path
Provider outages are the most common cause
Disable the feature flag for the affected feature
<30s to take effect; buys investigation time
Check model version; consider rollback to prior version
Model changes are most common latency cause
Disable feature flag immediately + page Trust & Safety
Tier 2 does not require immediate paging. The response is investigation-oriented:
Create a Jira ticket tagged with the SLO name and current burn rate.
Use the metrics to identify when the burn rate elevated and what changed at that time (deploy, config change, traffic shift).
Assess whether mitigation is needed now or whether a fix can be deployed in the next business day.
Monitor burn rate daily. If it rises to 14.4x, escalate to Tier 1 immediately.
No new feature deployments while budget is below 20% remaining.

The Tier 2 budget freeze (no new feature deploys while budget < 20%) is the most important policy enforcement mechanism in the error budget system. Without it, a degraded but not yet critical burn rate can be masked by a new deployment that pushes it to critical. The freeze forces the team to address the existing burn before adding new risk.
Tier 3 alerts do not require immediate action. They appear on the dashboard and are reviewed at the next standup or weekly review. The key actions are:
Note the budget consumption rate and compare to the same window last week.
Identify whether the cause is a known issue or a new degradation.
If the issue is trending toward Tier 2, create a proactive investigation ticket.
Deploy Gate: Enforcing the Budget Freeze

The budget freeze policy is only effective if it is enforced automatically. A manual policy that engineers must remember to check creates process gaps under deadline pressure. The deploy gate checks error budget status at deploy time and blocks or warns accordingly:
Deploy allowed but requires explicit approval from SRE lead
Deploy blocked; only emergency deploys allowed
Normal deploy process; no additional approval needed
The error budget report gives the team a weekly view of budget health across all SLOs, enabling proactive planning before a burn rate alert fires:

# ops/weekly_budget_report.py
# Generate the weekly error budget consumption report.
import requests, datetime

SLACK_WEBHOOK = 'https://hooks.slack.com/services/...'
PROMETHEUS = 'http://prometheus:9090'

def query(q: str) -> float:
    r = requests.get(f'{PROMETHEUS}/api/v1/query',
                     params={'query': q}, timeout=5)
    result = r.json()['data']['result']
    return float(result[0]['value'][1]) if result else float('nan')

def weekly_report():
    avail = query('sli:availability:ratio_1h')
    avail_budget_used = max(0, (0.999 - avail) / 0.001 * 100)
    avail_budget_left = max(0, 100 - avail_budget_used)

    eval_rate = query('sli:eval_pass_rate:avg_7d')
    eval_budget_used = max(0, (0.94 - eval_rate) / 0.06 * 100)
    eval_budget_left = max(0, 100 - eval_budget_used)

    safety = query(
        'rate(safety_flag_total[24h]) / rate(llm_request_total[24h])'
    )

    week = datetime.date.today().isocalendar()[1]
    msg = (
        f'*Weekly Error Budget Report — Week {week}*\n'
        f'Availability: {avail:.4%} | Budget left: {avail_budget_left:.1f}%\n'
        f'Eval pass rate: {eval_rate:.3%} | Budget left: {eval_budget_left:.1f}%\n'
        f'Safety flag rate: {safety:.5%} (limit: 0.05%)\n'
    )
    if avail_budget_left < 20 or eval_budget_left < 20:
        msg += ':warning: One or more budgets below 20% — review planned?'
    requests.post(SLACK_WEBHOOK, json={'text': msg})

if __name__ == '__main__':
    weekly_report()

The report is sent every Monday morning to the engineering and SRE channels. It serves as the primary mechanism for keeping the team aware of budget health without requiring them to open dashboards. If any budget is below 20%, the report includes a prompt for the team to identify whether a remediation plan exists.
Burn Rate Calibration for GenAI SLOs
The standard burn rate thresholds (14.4x, 6x, 3x) are calibrated for a 30-day availability SLO. For GenAI-specific SLOs with different windows, the thresholds must be recalculated:
Binary SLO; any breach is critical

For the 7-day eval pass rate SLO, a burn rate of 3.5x means the budget will be exhausted in exactly 2 days (7 / 3.5 = 2). This is more sensitive than the availability SLO because the eval window is shorter — even modest degradation consumes the budget quickly and warrants a page.

Burn rate alerts replace simple threshold alerts with a time-to-exhaustion framing: at the current failure rate, how long until the 30-day error budget is gone? Three tiers drive three different responses: 14.4x burn rate triggers an immediate P1 page (budget gone in 2 days), 6x triggers a ticket and watch with a deploy freeze below 20% remaining budget (budget gone in 5 days), and 3x appears on the dashboard for standup review (budget gone in 10 days). Each tier uses two windows simultaneously — a fast window to confirm the burn is active now, and a slow window to confirm it is sustained rather than a transient spike. The deploy gate enforces the budget freeze automatically at deploy time. For the 7-day eval pass rate SLO, the Tier 1 threshold is 3.5x rather than 14.4x because the shorter window makes the budget more sensitive to sustained degradation.
